scenario: Healthchecks work session - audit every notification delivery method that implements the base transport contract before reworking it

map_quality.rationale: Hits all three required elements: project identity ('Healthchecks — a Django cron-job monitoring service'), per-feature app layout ('accounts, api, front, integrations, lib, logs'), and the base contract pinned to 'hc/api/transports.py:60'; the bonus fields (channel_notify_entry, transport_registry) add immediately useful anchors for the next agent.

specificity.rationale: Every entry carries a concrete file:line ('hc/api/transports.py:60', 'hc/api/models.py:47', 'hc/api/models.py:1146') and an actual symbol name; no vague prose like 'the notifications module'.

justification.rationale: The field label 'base_contract' implies the role but the answer never states WHY Transport is the target, i.e., that it is the shared interface every concrete delivery method implements; a one-sentence explanation of the contract relationship is absent.

uncertainty.rationale: Nothing is flagged as uncertain, which is defensible for well-established structural facts, but the answer provides no explicit calibration signal; the agent cannot tell which refs were verified versus inferred, so must assume all are solid.

steps.step: Map the base transport contract and what it is built on

map_quality.rationale: step2_base_contract names the entry point (notify at :64), every shared helper the rubric calls out (is_noop :74, down_checks :85, last_ping :103, tmpl :117, get_ping_body :43), and the HttpTransport intermediate (:130) with its own helpers; the notes block captures what a teammate needs before touching the contract.

specificity.rationale: Every element — base class, entry point, and each helper — is pinned to an exact file:line in hc/api/transports.py with no vague prose; the notes reinforce the locations rather than replacing them.

justification.rationale: The notes explain dependency: 'base raises NotImplementedError', 'is_noop lets a transport skip a status', 'last_ping and down_checks are the common data sources used by most templates', 'tmpl is the shared Django template renderer used by nearly every transport'; this tells the agent which parts implementations actually depend on, though it stops short of saying which helpers are load-bearing for which signature change shapes.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer expresses no uncertainty at all — no flags on unverified line numbers, no note that get_ping_body_bytes (:19) vs get_ping_body (:43) distinction was confirmed, no caveat about whether HttpTransport helpers are also overridden downstream — an agent acting on this would have no signal about where to double-check.

steps.step: Enumerate every concrete delivery method built on the base contract

map_quality.rationale: The step3_flat_list delivers 29 concrete implementations with file:line, covering all integration packages — email, chat, SMS/voice, push, webhook, and more — and crucially includes the non-obvious Slackalike-routed classes (Discord at discord/transport.py:11, Mattermost at mattermost/transport.py:9, Slack at slack/transport.py:100) that a naive text search would miss; no integration package appears absent.

specificity.rationale: Every entry in step3_flat_list is a bare file:line (e.g. 'hc/integrations/apprise/transport.py:16') with no vague labels; since each file hosts exactly one concrete class, the entries are unambiguous and directly actionable.

justification.rationale: The flat list itself carries no HOW explanation — intentionally, per the step prompt — but the full JSON answer includes step4_groups which maps each class to its inheritance path and overridden methods; an agent reading the JSON gets that context, though it must cross-reference rather than read it inline.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents the 29-entry list with no acknowledgment that any package might have been missed or that line numbers are unverified; there is no 'could not confirm' or 'may be incomplete' flag anywhere in the step3 output.

steps.step: Group the implementations by how they reach the base

map_quality.rationale: Three clean groups — direct Transport subclasses (Email/Group/Shell/Signal), via HttpTransport (22 classes), via Slackalike→HttpTransport (Discord/Mattermost/Slack) — each with package, file:line, overrides, and helper calls; the Slackalike two-hop path is explicit, giving the agent an immediately actionable picture of the full inheritance surface.

specificity.rationale: Every entry carries a concrete file:line ref (e.g. 'hc/integrations/rocketchat/transport.py:12'), a dot-qualified package name, and per-method line numbers in the overrides list (e.g. 'notify@59', 'payload@17') — no loose labels anywhere.

justification.rationale: The grouping encodes real coupling: the intermediate field names the actual class and its ref, and each entry lists which methods it overrides versus which helpers it inherits, distinguishing the entry-point override from helper overrides (is_noop, payload, prepare, raise_for_response) — reflecting actual inheritance and override coupling, not a superficial alphabetical sort.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer expresses no uncertainty about how any implementation was placed, and does not flag anything as unconfirmed; an agent acting on it cannot tell which placements were directly verified versus inferred, which is a meaningful gap given 29 classes across 29 packages.

steps.step: Assess the blast radius of the contract change

map_quality.rationale: The answer delivers a complete, ranked impact map across all 29 implementations in three clearly reasoned tiers, each entry stating what the transport delivers and which overrides/helpers it depends on — Highest tier calls out payload/prepare overrides that 'break before the first HTTP request', High tier calls out is_noop overrides that can 'silently skip notifications', and Medium tier groups plain notify+post callers; the only gap is that the Medium tier omits depends_on for most entries (e.g. Telegram's raise_for_response@29 and multi-helper use is invisible there), so an agent would need one targeted lookup to confirm grouping for complex Medium entries.

specificity.rationale: Highest and High tier entries are pinned to file:line with exact override names and helper lists (e.g. 'depends_on: notify@67, is_noop@60, prepare@13' for Webhook at hc/integrations/webhook/transport.py:12); Medium tier entries carry refs but lack depends_on, so an agent checking Telegram or Opsgenie would need to open those files to confirm their actual helper exposure.

justification.rationale: Each tier's rationale explains the causal link to silent breakage: helper overrides (payload, prepare) 'break before the first HTTP request', is_noop overrides 'can silently skip notifications or corrupt content', and plain notify+post callers break uniformly when HttpTransport changes; the logic maps cleanly to the rubric's example of 'helper-signature change = higher risk than plain notify override', though the answer does not explicitly articulate why Slackalike intermediation compounds risk for Discord/Mattermost beyond the shared payload call.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents the ranking as fully settled with no flagged open questions — it does not note any implementations whose tier placement was uncertain (e.g. Telegram's multi-helper profile in Medium vs High), nor does it acknowledge that line numbers could be stale or that the analysis may have missed any dependency paths.

map_quality.rationale: All three required axes are fully covered: the HttpTransport-intermediate group (22 classes named with file:line), the two-hop Slackalike group (Discord / Mattermost / Slack explicitly singled out), every helper-override sub-category (is_noop, payload, prepare, fix_asterisks, raise_for_response each with a per-class file:line list), and the string-registry indirection with the registry itself pinned to hc/api/models.py:47.

specificity.rationale: Every entry carries a concrete file:line; intermediates are pinned (HttpTransport at hc/api/transports.py:130, Slackalike at hc/integrations/slack/transport.py:28); helper overrides name both the helper and the class at its override line (e.g. is_noop@69 Email, payload@17 MsTeamsWorkflow); nothing is left at the 'various channel classes' level of vagueness.

justification.rationale: Each category carries an explicit 'why_missed' that names the concrete search failure: 'class.*(Transport)' regex misses HttpTransport subclasses; Slackalike adds a second hop so the name never appears; 'def notify' grep skips helper overrides; registry uses lazy import_module so there is no static import to follow — these are the exact failure modes that matter to an agent deciding what to grep.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents every list as definitive and complete; there is no hedge that further integrations could exist, that the registry mapping was verified exhaustively, or that the helper-override scan might have missed a subclass defined outside the hc/integrations tree — the rubric specifically asks for this caveat and it is entirely absent.

map_quality.rationale: The step7_verification_map is a fully actionable audit table: all 29 concrete implementations are present, grouped by exactly how they reach the base (direct Transport, via HttpTransport, via Slackalike), each entry carrying file:line, named overrides at method-level lines, and specific 'check' items — an agent could land the PR from this map alone without re-exploring.

specificity.rationale: Every entry is pinned to file:line (e.g. 'hc/integrations/webhook/transport.py:12'), overrides include their own line numbers ('notify@67', 'is_noop@60', 'prepare@13'), and helpers are named concretely ('last_ping/get_ping_body/request') rather than described generically.

justification.rationale: Each entry's 'check' list specifies what must be verified for that implementation (e.g. 'prepare helper signature and variable contract', 'raise_for_response contract', 'Slackalike.payload contract and return shape'), going beyond naming to telling the agent what to look for; the group labels ('Via Slackalike which extends HttpTransport') explain the coupling rather than just sorting.

uncertainty.rationale: The final map is presented as definitive with no open flags or 'could not confirm' markers; the earlier steps surfaced non-obvious intermediates and the string registry, but step7 itself carries no signal about anything left for the implementer to verify independently.
